events.title: Landing Page Feedback
events.snippet: Ok yeah—this is a noticeable jump. Feels way more intentional now.You fixed the biggest issue: 👉 it no longer screams “generic AI box”Now it’s starting to feel like: “this is about my mind, not a tool” 🔥 What you improved (and it shows)1. “What thought have you been circling ar
events.full_text:
Ok yeah—this is a noticeable jump. Feels way more intentional now.You fixed the biggest issue:
👉 it no longer screams “generic AI box”Now it’s starting to feel like:
“this is about my mind, not a tool”
🔥 What you improved (and it shows)1. “What thought have you been circling around?”This is 🔥
more specific than “thinking”
implies repetition → depth
feels slightly personal (good tension)
And making it adaptive?
👉 smart. Keeps it alive + reduces static feel.2. Example inside input
“startups are better than jobs”
Perfect direction.
it’s a belief, not a question
slightly opinionated
easy to relate to
Now users get it instantly.3. Right-side explanation boxThis actually helps now.Before: boring
Now: contextualBut still slightly “explainer mode” (we’ll fix that).⚠️ What still feels a bit off1. “Ready.” is still dead spaceYou’ve improved everything… and then:
Ready.
kills the vibe 💀Replace with something that builds anticipation:
“Tracing patterns…”
“Looking for what shaped this…”
“Connecting with your activity…”
Tiny change → big perception shift.2. Explanation box = still too safeCurrent:
“Memact helps you see where your thoughts may be coming from…”
This is correct but not compelling.Make it hit harder:Instead:
Most thoughts don’t start with you.
Memact shows what shaped them.
Or:
You didn’t form this in isolation.
We show what influenced it.
Now it feels like insight, not documentation.3. Still slightly “tool-like”You’re close, but one small addition would push it:Add a micro-line above or below input:
“Not a question. Just a thought.”
or
“Type what’s on your mind. Not what you want answered.”
This completely breaks chatbot expectations.⚡ One high-impact addition (do this next)👉 Clickable thought chipsUnder the input:
“I’m falling behind”
“I need to prove myself”
“This decision feels risky”
Clickable → fills inputWhy this matters:
reduces friction
shows emotional + real use cases
instantly increases engagement
🧠 Bigger product signal (important)Right now your UI says:
“we connect thoughts to activity”
Good.Next level is:
“we reveal patterns you didn’t notice”
That’s the shift from:
tool → insight engine
🧩 Honest progression
First version → generic
Second version → clean tool
This version → starting to feel like a product with a POV
You’re moving in the right direction fast.⚡ Final tweak stack (quick wins)Do these and it levels up instantly:
Replace “Ready.”
Sharpen right-side copy (less polite, more punch)
Add 3–5 clickable thought examples
Add “not a question” micro-copy
🧠 Final verdictThis is no longer “just a project UI”It’s now:
something people might actually try
Next battle:
the output screen (that’s where you win or lose everything)
If you want, next I’ll design the exact output experience that creates that
“yeah… that didn’t come from nowhere”
moment.

events.title: Landing Page Feedback
events.snippet: Ok yeah—this is a noticeable jump. Feels way more intentional now.You fixed the biggest issue: 👉 it no longer screams “generic AI box”Now it’s starting to feel like: “this is about my mind, not a tool” 🔥 What you improved (and it shows)1. “What thought have you been circling ar
events.full_text:
Ok yeah—this is a noticeable jump. Feels way more intentional now.You fixed the biggest issue:
👉 it no longer screams “generic AI box”Now it’s starting to feel like:
“this is about my mind, not a tool”
🔥 What you improved (and it shows)1. “What thought have you been circling around?”This is 🔥
more specific than “thinking”
implies repetition → depth
feels slightly personal (good tension)
And making it adaptive?
👉 smart. Keeps it alive + reduces static feel.2. Example inside input
“startups are better than jobs”
Perfect direction.
it’s a belief, not a question
slightly opinionated
easy to relate to
Now users get it instantly.3. Right-side explanation boxThis actually helps now.Before: boring
Now: contextualBut still slightly “explainer mode” (we’ll fix that).⚠️ What still feels a bit off1. “Ready.” is still dead spaceYou’ve improved everything… and then:
Ready.
kills the vibe 💀Replace with something that builds anticipation:
“Tracing patterns…”
“Looking for what shaped this…”
“Connecting with your activity…”
Tiny change → big perception shift.2. Explanation box = still too safeCurrent:
“Memact helps you see where your thoughts may be coming from…”
This is correct but not compelling.Make it hit harder:Instead:
Most thoughts don’t start with you.
Memact shows what shaped them.
Or:
You didn’t form this in isolation.
We show what influenced it.
Now it feels like insight, not documentation.3. Still slightly “tool-like”You’re close, but one small addition would push it:Add a micro-line above or below input:
“Not a question. Just a thought.”
or
“Type what’s on your mind. Not what you want answered.”
This completely breaks chatbot expectations.⚡ One high-impact addition (do this next)👉 Clickable thought chipsUnder the input:
“I’m falling behind”
“I need to prove myself”
“This decision feels risky”
Clickable → fills inputWhy this matters:
reduces friction
shows emotional + real use cases
instantly increases engagement
🧠 Bigger product signal (important)Right now your UI says:
“we connect thoughts to activity”
Good.Next level is:
“we reveal patterns you didn’t notice”
That’s the shift from:
tool → insight engine
🧩 Honest progression
First version → generic
Second version → clean tool
This version → starting to feel like a product with a POV
You’re moving in the right direction fast.⚡ Final tweak stack (quick wins)Do these and it levels up instantly:
Replace “Ready.”
Sharpen right-side copy (less polite, more punch)
Add 3–5 clickable thought examples
Add “not a question” micro-copy
🧠 Final verdictThis is no longer “just a project UI”It’s now:
something people might actually try
Next battle:
the output screen (that’s where you win or lose everything)
If you want, next I’ll design the exact output experience that creates that
“yeah… that didn’t come from nowhere”
moment.

events.snippet: Yeah that’s actually better than chips if you execute it right.Chips are static. What you’re building is: context-aware suggestions That’s a higher tier.🧠 But here’s the catch (important)Most people mess this up.If your “digital activity suggestions” feel like: generic autocompl
events.full_text:
Yeah that’s actually better than chips if you execute it right.Chips are static.
What you’re building is:
context-aware suggestions
That’s a higher tier.🧠 But here’s the catch (important)Most people mess this up.If your “digital activity suggestions” feel like:
generic autocomplete
→ it’s worse than chipsIf it feels like:
“how tf did it know I was thinking this”
→ now you’ve got something special⚡ What makes your version work vs fail❌ Bad version (what to avoid)
“startups vs jobs”
“career decisions”
“productivity tips”
This feels like:
Google suggestions
Dead.✅ Good version (what you should aim for)Suggestions should feel:
personal
slightly specific
slightly uncomfortable / relatable
Like:
“I should be doing more right now”
“I’m not building enough compared to others”
“this decision might be a mistake”
Now user thinks:
“yeah… that’s actually me”
🔥 How to make your feature hit harder1. Don’t label it as “suggestions”Bad:
Suggestions
Better:
“You might be thinking…”
“This has been on your mind?”
“Patterns we’re noticing…”
Framing matters a lot.2. Timing mattersDon’t show them immediately.Do this:
user clicks input
slight delay (300–500ms)
then suggestions appear
Feels less mechanical, more “thinking”3. Keep it minimalMax 3 suggestions.Too many = noise
Few = intentional4. Make them clickable (obviously)But also:
→ when clicked, slightly animate fill into inputSmall UX polish = big perceived intelligence🧠 Big insightYou’re accidentally building one of the most powerful parts of your product:
predicting thoughts before user types them
If this works well, it becomes:
retention driver
shareable moment
“this app gets me” feeling
⚠️ One warningDon’t fake it.If suggestions aren’t truly based on activity (or at least convincingly tied):
users will feel it instantly
And then trust = gone.🧩 Final takeYou were gonna add chips (good)You replaced it with:
activity-based thought suggestions
That’s:
objectively a better product decision
Now execution decides everything.If you want, I can help you design:
exactly how those suggestions should look
and how to make them feel creepy accurate (in a good way)

events.title: Memact/Website: Memact Interface. Under construction.
events.snippet: Version: v0.0 Memact helps you see where your thoughts may be coming from. You type a thought. Memact looks at what you read, watch, search, and revisit. Then it shows links that may have shaped that thought. Why this matters: it can help you catch one-sided thinking it can help
events.full_text:
Version: v0.0
Memact helps you see where your thoughts may be coming from.
You type a thought.
Memact looks at what you read, watch, search, and revisit.
Then it shows links that may have shaped that thought.
Why this matters:
it can help you catch one-sided thinking
it can help you make better decisions
it can help you understand why some thoughts or feelings keep showing up
it can help you explain your ideas more clearly
How Memact Works
Capture -> Inference -> Schema -> Website -> Influence / Origin
Capture
Saves useful activity from your device.
Inference
Finds the main meaning in that activity.
Schema
Notices patterns that keep repeating.
Website
Lets you type a thought and see the result.
Influence
Shows what may have shaped the thought over time.
Origin
Shows what may have first introduced the thought.
Infrastructure Contract
Website is only one Memact client.
The same backend shape should also work for Android and later API explanations.
The shared contract lives here:
docs/platform-contract.md
The important rule is simple:
AI can help explain the wording later, but the evidence must already come from deterministic Memact engines.
First-Time Setup
Memact does not interrupt the user right away.
Setup starts when the user first tries to use search.
If Capture is not installed yet:
Download the extension zip.
Unzip it into a folder.
Open chrome://extensions or edge://extensions.
Turn on Developer Mode.
Click Load unpacked.
Pick the unzipped folder.
After that, Memact can ask to import some recent activity from this device so it does not start empty.
If the user allows it, Memact starts building first suggestions.
If the user skips it, Memact waits for new activity from then on.
If the user changes their mind later, the Settings button lets them turn local import on.
Website does not keep its own extension source code.
Capture is the only extension codebase.
Website only serves the packaged zip that comes from Capture.
Run Locally
You need:
Node.js 20+
npm 10+
Install:
npm install
Run:
npm run dev
Build:
npm run build
Preview:
npm run preview
Extension Zip
Build the extension in Capture first:
cd ..\capture
npm install
npm run package-extension
Then sync that packaged zip into Website:
cd ..\interface
npm run sync-capture-zip
The website then serves that same packaged zip here:
public/memact-extension.zip
Search Setup
The website already includes the main Google search basics:
canonical URL
robots.txt
sitemap.xml
search metadata
structured data
favicon and web manifest
One thing still has to be done by the real site owner:
Google Search Console verification.
After verification, the normal next steps are:
Request indexing if needed
Repositories
Capture
Inference
Schema
Origin
Influence
Website
License
See LICENSE.

context_profile.fullText:
Version: v0.0
Memact helps you see where your thoughts may be coming from.
You type a thought.
Memact looks at what you read, watch, search, and revisit.
Then it shows links that may have shaped that thought.
Why this matters:
it can help you catch one-sided thinking
it can help you make better decisions
it can help you understand why some thoughts or feelings keep showing up
it can help you explain your ideas more clearly
How Memact Works
Capture -> Inference -> Schema -> Website -> Influence / Origin
Capture
Saves useful activity from your device.
Inference
Finds the main meaning in that activity.
Schema
Notices patterns that keep repeating.
Website
Lets you type a thought and see the result.
Influence
Shows what may have shaped the thought over time.
Origin
Shows what may have first introduced the thought.
Infrastructure Contract
Website is only one Memact client.
The same backend shape should also work for Android and later API explanations.
The shared contract lives here:
docs/platform-contract.md
The important rule is simple:
AI can help explain the wording later, but the evidence must already come from deterministic Memact engines.
First-Time Setup
Memact does not interrupt the user right away.
Setup starts when the user first tries to use search.
If Capture is not installed yet:
Download the extension zip.
Unzip it into a folder.
Open chrome://extensions or edge://extensions.
Turn on Developer Mode.
Click Load unpacked.
Pick the unzipped folder.
After that, Memact can ask to import some recent activity from this device so it does not start empty.
If the user allows it, Memact starts building first suggestions.
If the user skips it, Memact waits for new activity from then on.
If the user changes their mind later, the Settings button lets them turn local import on.
Website does not keep its own extension source code.
Capture is the only extension codebase.
Website only serves the packaged zip that comes from Capture.
Run Locally
You need:
Node.js 20+
npm 10+
Install:
npm install
Run:
npm run dev
Build:
npm run build
Preview:
npm run preview
Extension Zip
Build the extension in Capture first:
cd ..\capture
npm install
npm run package-extension
Then sync that packaged zip into Website:
cd ..\interface
npm run sync-capture-zip
The website then serves that same packaged zip here:
public/memact-extension.zip
Search Setup
The website already includes the main Google search basics:
canonical URL
robots.txt
sitemap.xml
search metadata
structured data
favicon and web manifest
One thing still has to be done by the real site owner:
Google Search Console verification.
After verification, the normal next steps are:
Request indexing if needed
Repositories
Capture
Inference
Schema
Origin
Influence
Website
License
See LICENSE.

ProjectDefault Gemini ProjectTime Range28 DaysOverviewNo data availableUntitledThis chart is described by one or more grids. A grid row represents a time series. A grid column corresponds to a timestamp. By default, charts hide most columns. To display the hidden columns next to the activated column, use the Read previous item (up arrow) key until you access the column header, and then use the Activate button (enter) key. Unpinning the X axis cursor allows the cursor to follow the mouse while hovering over the chart. The X axis cursor will always be pinned while using keyboard navigation to indicate that the values reflected in the tooltip or the legend are associated with the position of the X axis cursor.
Navigate this grid using arrow keys. Page Up and Page Down moves focus up and down by 10 rows. Home and End moves focus to the first and last cell in the current row. Ctrl+Home and Ctrl+End moves focus to the first and last cell of the grid.
